programs.name: Maryland Durable Medical Equipment Reuse Program (DME)
programs.tagline: Get free wheelchairs, walkers, hospital beds, and other medical equipment for your parent — no income limits required.

localEntities.name: Satellite pickup sites statewide (call 240-230-8000 to confirm locations and stock).

programs.intro:
If your parent needs medical equipment like walkers, wheelchairs, shower chairs, or hospital beds, Maryland's DME Reuse Program provides these items for free. Because insurance often denies or delays coverage for durable medical equipment, this program fills the gap with gently-used, sanitized equipment donated by other families.

The program has no income limits or age requirements — any Maryland resident can qualify. Your parent can borrow equipment for unlimited time, including canes, crutches, walkers, rollators, shower chairs, bedside commodes, manual and power wheelchairs, hospital beds, and patient lifts. Because inventory depends on donations, you'll need to call ahead to check availability.

Your parent's doctor, physical therapist, or other healthcare provider must complete the request form. Power wheelchairs and scooters require approval specifically from a physical or occupational therapist.

otherRequirements: Healthcare professional (PT, OT, MD, DO, PA, or CRNP) must initiate request
Must have exhausted all other means of obtaining equipment (insurance, other programs)
Power wheelchairs and scooters require physical or occupational therapist approval only

applicationGuide.summary: Healthcare provider completes online form, then you're contacted to schedule pickup if equipment is available.

steps.title: Have healthcare provider complete request form
steps.description: Your parent's doctor, PT, OT, PA, or nurse practitioner must fill out either the Basic DME Request Form (for simple items like walkers, canes) or Complex DME Request Form (for wheelchairs, hospital beds, lifts). Power equipment requires PT or OT signature only.

steps.title: Submit completed form
steps.description: Email scanned form to dme.mdoa@maryland.gov (no cell phone photos), submit online at onestop.md.gov, or mail to 11701 Crain Highway, Cheltenham, MD 20623.

steps.title: Wait for availability confirmation
steps.description: Program staff will contact you via phone or email to notify if requested equipment is in stock and schedule pickup.

steps.title: Pick up equipment
steps.description: Arrange pickup at main Cheltenham location or satellite site. No delivery available — you must pick up in person.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Not specified; contacted after review if equipment available
applicationGuide.tip: Call 240-230-8000 before applying to check if your needed equipment is currently in stock — saves time on paperwork.

offices.name: Satellite Locations
offices.address: Multiple sites statewide — call to confirm locations and stock availability

contentSections.text: Limited inventory based on donations. Call 240-230-8000 to verify equipment availability before submitting paperwork. No delivery — you must arrange pickup.
Program is designed for families who have exhausted insurance options. If your parent's insurance denied coverage or has long delays, keep that documentation — it helps demonstrate need.

faqs.question: What if my parent's insurance covers some DME but not everything they need?
faqs.answer: You can still apply for items insurance doesn't cover. The program requires you exhaust other options first, but that doesn't mean insurance must cover everything — just that you've tried available options.

faqs.question: How long can my parent keep the borrowed equipment?
faqs.answer: Unlimited borrowing time. There's no requirement to return equipment by a certain date, though the program appreciates returns when equipment is no longer needed so other families can use it.

faqs.question: Can I apply directly, or does it have to be the doctor?
faqs.answer: A healthcare professional must complete and sign the form — you cannot submit it yourself. This includes doctors, PAs, nurse practitioners, physical therapists, or occupational therapists. For power wheelchairs and scooters, only PTs or OTs can make the request.

faqs.question: What happens if the equipment we need isn't available?
faqs.answer: The program will contact you only if requested equipment is in stock. If it's not available, you likely won't hear back. Call 240-230-8000 periodically to check if new donations have come in.

faqs.question: Is there a limit to how many items my parent can borrow?
faqs.answer: No specific limit mentioned in program materials. Requests are evaluated based on medical need and availability. The program aims to help families who have no other options for obtaining necessary equipment.

faqs.question: What condition is the used equipment in?
faqs.answer: All equipment is gently-used, sanitized, and refurbished before distribution. The program only provides equipment that meets safety and functionality standards, though it may show normal signs of previous use.

programs.name: Maryland Community First Choice (CFC) and Home and Community-Based Services (HCBS) Waivers
programs.tagline: Get personal care assistance for your parent at home — no nursing home needed if they qualify for Medicaid.
programs.intro:
If your parent needs help with bathing, dressing, or daily activities but wants to stay home, Maryland's Community First Choice (CFC) provides personal care assistance through Medicaid. Because CFC is an entitlement program, there's no waiting list — your parent gets services immediately if they qualify.

CFC covers fewer services than HCBS Waivers but has the advantage of immediate access. Your parent must already qualify for Maryland Medicaid and receive specific benefits like SSI or be enrolled in certain Medicaid programs. Income limit: $16,105/year for a single person if they're not on Medicare.

summary: Receive SSI, TCA, or enrolled in Medicaid Waiver
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Need for personal care assistance as assessed by local health department (help with bathing, dressing, eating, or mobility)
otherRequirements: Must receive SSI, TCA, foster care benefits, or be enrolled in a Medicaid Waiver
Cannot qualify with only Medicare, private insurance, QMB, or SLMB

applicationGuide.summary: Contact your local health department for a medical assessment — processing time not specified by the state.

steps.title: Contact Local Health Department
steps.description: Call your local health department to request an assessment for Community First Choice services.

steps.title: Complete Medical Assessment
steps.description: A supports planner will assess your parent's need for personal care assistance and create a plan of service.

steps.title: Medicaid Staff Review
steps.description: Maryland Medicaid staff reviews the assessment and plan of service to determine eligibility and service hours.

steps.title: Services Begin
steps.description: If approved, personal care services start immediately — no waiting list for CFC.

applicationGuide.waitlist: No waitlist for CFC — services begin immediately if eligible. HCBS Waivers do have waitlists.
applicationGuide.tip: If denied, you have only 10 days to appeal to continue receiving benefits during the appeal process.

tiers.name: Community First Choice (CFC)
tiers.description: Personal care assistance and limited home services
tiers.coverage: No waitlist, immediate services, fewer service options

tiers.name: HCBS Waivers
tiers.incomeLimit: Higher income limits possible depending on waiver
tiers.coverage: Waitlist required, more extensive services available

categories.name: Financial
items: Proof of income (Social Security award letters, pay stubs)

categories.name: Medical
items: Current Medicaid card or eligibility verification
Medical records showing need for daily assistance
List of current medications and medical conditions

categories.name: Benefits
items: TCA or other public benefit documentation

contentSections.text: CFC has stricter income limits than some HCBS Waivers. If your parent's income is over $16,105/year and they're not on Medicare, they may still qualify through certain Medicaid Waivers with higher income limits.

Not all Medicaid recipients qualify for CFC. Your parent must receive specific benefits like SSI or be enrolled in certain Medicaid programs — having only Medicare or private insurance won't qualify them.

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their house if they qualify for CFC?
faqs.answer: Yes. The primary home is exempt from asset limits for CFC eligibility, regardless of value.

faqs.question: What if my parent's income is just over the $16,105 limit?
faqs.answer: They may still qualify through certain Medicaid Waivers like Community Options, which have higher income limits. SSI recipients automatically qualify regardless of the income limit.

faqs.question: How many hours of care will my parent receive?
faqs.answer: Hours are determined individually by a supports planner through a plan of service assessment based on your parent's specific needs for daily activities.

faqs.question: What's the difference between CFC and HCBS Waivers?
faqs.answer: CFC has no waitlist and provides immediate services but offers fewer service options. HCBS Waivers have waitlists but provide more comprehensive services and may allow higher incomes.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent is denied CFC services?
faqs.answer: You have 10 days to appeal the denial. Mail appeals to Maryland Department of Health, Office of Health Services, 201 W. Preston St., 1st Floor, Baltimore, MD 21201.

faqs.question: Does my parent need to be on Medicare to qualify?
faqs.answer: No. In fact, the $16,105 income limit applies specifically to non-Medicare eligible individuals. Medicare-only coverage won't qualify them for CFC.

programs.name: Maryland Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly (PACE)
programs.tagline: If your parent needs nursing home level care but wants to stay at home, PACE provides all medical and long-term care services through one team.
programs.intro:
If your parent is 55+ and needs extensive daily help with bathing, dressing, eating, or walking, Maryland PACE provides all their medical care, prescriptions, and long-term care services through one coordinated team. Because PACE covers everything under one program, your parent won't need to coordinate between separate doctors, specialists, or home care providers — one team manages it all.

The catch: your parent must receive ALL medical care exclusively through PACE providers. They cannot see outside doctors or use other Medicare services. About 90% of participants qualify for both Medicare and Medicaid, which covers the full cost. Private pay runs $7,000+ per month.

Currently, PACE is only available in specific Baltimore-area zip codes through PACE of West Baltimore. Your parent must live in the service area and be certified as needing nursing home level care, but be able to safely remain at home with PACE support.

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must be certified as needing nursing home level care (requiring extensive assistance with bathing, grooming, toileting, walking, transferring, or eating)
otherRequirements: Must live in approved Baltimore-area zip codes
Cannot be enrolled in Medicare Advantage or Medicare prescription drug plans
Must agree to receive ALL medical care exclusively through PACE
Must be safely maintainable at home with PACE support

applicationGuide.summary: Contact PACE of West Baltimore directly for enrollment — processing time not specified in available information.

steps.title: Contact PACE of West Baltimore
steps.description: Call or visit their enrollment office to start the process. They serve specific Baltimore-area zip codes only.

steps.title: Complete medical release forms
steps.description: PACE will obtain your parent's medical records and check Medicare/Medicaid eligibility status.

steps.title: Clinical assessment
steps.description: PACE team evaluates whether your parent needs nursing home level care and can be safely maintained at home.

steps.title: Sign enrollment agreement
steps.description: If approved, your parent agrees to receive all medical care exclusively through PACE providers.

applicationGuide.tip: Confirm your parent's zip code is in the service area before starting — PACE of West Baltimore only serves specific Baltimore-area locations.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts for Medicaid Coverage
excluded: Burial funds up to certain limits

categories.name: Residency
items: Proof of residence in service area zip code

contentSections.text: Your parent cannot use any outside medical providers once enrolled in PACE — no outside doctors, specialists, or prescription plans. PACE becomes their sole source of all medical care.
About 90% of PACE participants qualify for both Medicare and Medicaid, which covers the full cost. Without Medicaid, private pay is approximately $7,000+ per month.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep seeing her current doctor if she enrolls in PACE?
faqs.answer: No. Once enrolled, your parent must receive ALL medical care exclusively through PACE providers. They cannot see outside doctors, specialists, or use other Medicare services.

faqs.question: What if Mom's income is just over the Medicaid limit of $2,901/month?
faqs.answer: She can still enroll in PACE, but without Medicaid coverage, she'll pay approximately $7,000+ per month privately. Only about 10% of participants pay privately.

faqs.question: Can Mom leave PACE if she doesn't like it?
faqs.answer: Yes. Participants can leave the program at any time for any reason and return to regular Medicare/Medicaid services.

faqs.question: Does PACE cover prescription medications?
faqs.answer: Yes. PACE provides comprehensive coverage including all prescription drugs, medical care, preventive services, and hospitalization through their coordinated care team.

faqs.question: What zip codes does PACE serve in Maryland?
faqs.answer: Currently only PACE of West Baltimore operates, serving these Baltimore-area zip codes: 21201, 21207, 21209, 21210, 21211, 21212, 21215, 21216, 21223, 21225, 21226, 21228, 21229, 21230, 21239, 21251, 21287.

faqs.question: Does Mom's house count toward the $2,000 asset limit?
faqs.answer: No. The primary home and one automobile are exempt from Medicaid asset limits, regardless of value. Other assets like savings accounts and investments count toward the $2,000 limit.
